name: Arbitova
tagline: Dispute resolution layer for agent-to-agent commerce.
description: Open protocol that produces verifiable `disputed` attestations for the A2A trust surface. Complements reputation + discovery layers; does not replace them.

protocol.role: produces `disputed` relations that attestation-layer extensions can consume

networks.name: base-mainnet
networks.note: Mainnet deploy paused pending standards positioning on A2A trust surface.

defenses.name: structural-prompt-injection-isolation
defenses.description: Untrusted evidence fields are XML-wrapped with zero-width-space escaping of closing tags, so neither party can break out of their evidence frame to address the arbiter directly.

defenses.name: content-hash-verification-keccak256
defenses.description: Delivery artifact is keccak256-hashed and compared against the on-chain hash before the arbiter pipeline sees it. A mismatch short-circuits the arbiter to a deterministic verdict.

trustAssumption.statement: You don't trust Arbitova — you reconstruct the verdict. Evidence hashes are on-chain, content-hash verification is deterministic, arbiter reasoning is public. A third party can recompute every step.
trustAssumption.boundedTo: arbiter reasoning was sound given the evidence it saw

knownLimitations: single-arbiter — no consensus layer yet; multi-arbiter selection on roadmap
real commercial case volume is still being bootstrapped

a2aIntegration.pattern: A2A extension — produces attestation-shape data for trust-layer extensions to consume

optimisticOracleFallback.style: UMA-style, for high-value cases where 2h SLA is relaxed

whyNotKlerosOrUMA.kleros: ~$60 floor too heavy for sub-$100 agent tasks; juror model does not handle structured evidence (content hashes, JSON artifacts) natively.
whyNotKlerosOrUMA.uma: 48h dispute window does not fit agent SLAs. UMA-style optimistic-oracle fallback is on our roadmap as a backstop for high-value cases, not the primary path.
